meta.title: DailyMinz
meta.subtitle: Record my road to the knowledge.
meta.author: Kawashima Iwami

pages.title: Abouts
pages.text: Where is this?Welcome to DailyMinz, my personal blog. You can find all kinds of knowledge, feelings, observations and insights here. If you want, you can even contribute to the improvement of this blog on GitHub! This blog is not limited to the content of a specific field, but is dedicated to recording the bits and pieces of my learning and thinking, in order to leave a trace for my spiritual journey and provide a little insight into this still chaotic world. This blog attempts to innovate on the traditional form. You can participate in the construction of this blog at any time through the GitHub community, provide your thoughts and insights, and inspire those who come after you. Community: https://github.com/StonePick/DailyMinz Who are you?I call myself Kawashima “StonePick” Iwami, 川島岩海. Visit my GitHub repo. Contact me E-mail: kstonire@gmail.comDiscord: StonePickGitHub: StonePick Copyright StatementAll original articles in this blog retain all copyrights and are licensed under the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license. The copyright of other content belongs to the original author and is licensed under their own licenses. This blog is driven by the Hexo framework. The framework is licensed under the CC BY 4.0 license. This blog uses the Fluid theme. This theme is licensed under the GNU General Public License v3.0. If the content quoted in this blog infringes your rights, please inform us by e-mail in time. We will remove the infringing content as soon as possible after verification. Copyright 2024 DailyMinz by StonePick This essay was generated by Google Translation. The Simplified Chinese edition prevails if conflicts exist.

posts.title: Multilingual Support Completed
posts.excerpt: This blog has completed multilingual construction. Readers can click the "🌐" button in the navigation bar to switch languages. I will try my best to ensure that blog posts in various languages ​​are updated synchronously. If you find that an article does not have a corresponding language version, you can use the corresponding template to publish an issue in our GitHub community, or you can also help translate.
posts.text: This blog has completed multilingual construction. Readers can click the “🌐” button in the navigation bar to switch languages. I will try my best to ensure that blog posts in various languages ​​are updated synchronously. If you find that an article does not have a corresponding language version, you can use the corresponding template to publish an issue in our GitHub community, or you can also help translate. The multilingual support (i18n) of the Hexo framework itself only provides internationalization of menus, etc., and cannot achieve multilingual support for blog content. Some themes have this function built in, but the Fluid theme used in this site is unfortunately not on this list. Therefore, I can only find relevant solutions on the Internet. I will record my findings and solution steps in this article, hoping to help other readers who are willing to build an internationalized blog. Maintain multiple sets of Hexo projects, integrate other languages ​​in the main language public folder (the chosen of this site)This method was discovered in the blog “Brando的自留地”. Since the original text (web archive) just introduces the solution available when using Fluid theme, and the principle is easy to understand, this site chose this method to achieve the internationalization of the blog. Preliminary preparationAccording to the idea of ​​the original text, copy the files of the main site (except node_modules) multiple times, and each copy corresponds to a language version. For example, this site supports three languages, ​​​​Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese (Taiwan) and English, that is, save the original Simplified Chinese project in two copies. In this way, the local project source file directory of this blog site presents the following structure: 1234DailyMinz |- en |- main-zh-CN |- zh-TW Here, the directory naming of each language version is actually not important. In theory, you can even name it randomly as long as you know it. In order to highlight the main language status of zh-CN, I added the prefix main- to it to distinguish it. Execute npm install in the newly added language branch to install dependencies. Then, copy the articles under main-zh-CN/source to other language branches and translate them according to the target language. This completes the basic steps of “extralocalization”. Language configurationUnder each language branch, modify the site configuration _config.yml and the language configuration of the theme to adapt it to the current language. _config.yml： 12# Sitelanguage: <corresponding language> Fluid’s Theme Language Configuration affects the text that comes with the theme<span class=”hint–top hint–rounded” aria-label=”See the description of the “Languages” section of Fluid official documentation for details: “Setting a different language in the site config will change the text of the theme.””>1, which is normally pre-configured. If you want to add your own customized content to the theme, you need to remember to add it in the language configuration file. For example, I added a “Community” link to the navigation bar, and the English language configuration file (en.yml) needs to include: 12community: menu: 'Community' Other language branches are handled similarly. Switch LanguageThe language switch button for this blog is set in the navigation bar, represented by a “🌐” symbol. In the theme configuration file, find the settings for the navigation bar. _config.fluid.yml： Sic erat scriptum. 12345678910111213# 导航栏的相关配置# Navigation barnavbar: # ... # 导航栏菜单，可自行增减，key 用来关联 languages/*.yml，如不存在关联则显示 key 本身的值；icon 是 css class，可以省略；增加 name 可以强制显示指定名称 # Navigation bar menu. `key` is used to associate languages/*.yml. If there is no association, the value of `key` itself will be displayed; if `icon` is a css class, it can be omitted; adding `name` can force the display of the specified name menu: - { key: "home", link: "/", icon: "iconfont icon-home-fill" } - { key: "about", link: "/about/", icon: "iconfont icon-user-fill" } - { key: "archive", link: "/archives/", icon: "iconfont icon-archive-fill" } - { key: "category", link: "/categories/", icon: "iconfont icon-category-fill" } - { key: "tag", link: "/tags/", icon: "iconfont icon-tags-fill" } # - { key: "links", link: "/links/", icon: "iconfont icon-link-fill" } I added the following code under menu: 12345678- { key: "lang", submenu: [ { key: "zh-CN", name: "简体中文", link: "../" }, { key: "zh-TW", name: "正體中文", link: "../zh-TW/" }, { key: "en", name: "English", link: "../en/" } ] } Use the multi-level menu to switch languages. Note the link part of each submenu. In addition, remember to add the mapping in the corresponding language configuration file. zh-CN.yml： 12lang: menu: '🌐' Integrate other language articlesNext, we are ready to deploy the site. Generate public files under each language branch, switch to the main language branch, and create two new folders, en and zh-TW, under main-zh-CN/public. Note that since the addresses of each language branch are hard-coded in the theme configuration, these two folders must be named according to the set path. Copy the public files under the other two language branches respectively. Then, deploy directly. We can find that the public file generated by Hexo is the entire website structure itself. Switching to other languages ​​means accessing the subdirectory of the website root directory (locally, public), that is, /en/ or /zh-TW/. If we jump out of the internationalization construction itself, we can also use this to perform more free and personalized operations. For example, when you write an html document by hand and want to connect it to a website built with the Hexo framework, you can start directly from public. For example, if I want to use a homepage instead of the one generated by Fluid, I can replace public/index.html; if I want to add a custom login/subscription page, I can create a new accounts/subscribe subdirectory in it, and access it in the browser. The URL is https://example.com/accounts/page.html. But please note that the source files of these custom projects must be saved elsewhere, otherwise, when the hexo clean command is used to clean the cache, all the hard work under public will be burned. Regarding this “free” custom operation, as well as the topic of related automated maintenance and deployment, I will continue to study related solutions. Currently, I am considering writing a Python program to implement these operations. DisadvantagesThe most obvious one is: maintaining multiple sets of projects at the same time, the operation is complicated, and it is easy to “lose one thing while taking care of another”. Especially when changing the blog configuration file, it is more likely to have problems. Another one is the common problem of multi-language sites: keeping the synchronous update of multi-language content is indeed a very test of the blogger’s ability and perseverance. Maintain a single Hexo project and deploy multiple repositoriesThe related page of The blog “井说” (Web Archive) introduces another method and provides an automated deployment solution. This multi-language solution only requires maintaining one set of Hexo projects, but it still requires managing configuration files in different languages. The author takes the approach of directly creating directories for storing articles in different languages ​​in source to distinguish articles in different languages. In automated deployment, files belonging to different language branches are pushed to different repositories respectively, and the address feature of GitHub Pages is used to distinguish different language versions. The essential difference between this solution and the previous one is that the previous solution puts “multiple GitHub repositories” locally, that is, multiple Hexo projects; while this solution hosts each project on GitHub in an automated way, and only one set of projects is kept locally to accommodate files of each language branch. Since this blog does not adopt this solution, it will not be elaborated in detail. However, the original article ends with an idea of ​​using Google Translate’s API to translate articles during deployment. As a “lazy” way to handle multi-language content, this idea is worth exploring (laughs). Modify the homepage display methodIf you use the hexo-generator-i18n plugin to implement multilingual content directly by creating a multilingual directory under _post, there will be a fatal problem: the blog homepage will display all articles, regardless of language. To address this problem, an article on the blog “Linvis” (Web Archive) proposed a way to directly modify the theme layout. Compared with the previous one, this solution is more technical. This solution requires the hexo-generator-i18n plugin to distinguish the article language by inserting the lang parameter in the article’s front-matter. The author pointed out that it is necessary to modify the theme’s layout/index.ejs and add the following code: 123456789101112131415161718192021222324252627282930313233<main class="app-body"> <% var currentURL = page.path.split('/') %> <% if (currentURL.length === 1) { %> <% var lang = page.lang %> <% } else { %> <% var lang = currentURL.shift() %> <% } %> <% page.posts.each(function(post) { %> <% if (lang == post.lang) { %> <article class="article-card"> <h2 class="article-head"> <a href="<%- url_for(post.path) %>"><%- post.title %></a> </h2> <p class="article-date"><%- date(post.date, "LL") %></p> <% if (post.tags && post.tags.length) { %> <%- partial('_partial/tag', { tags: post.tags }) %> <% } %> <div class="article-summary"> <% if (post.excerpt) { %> <%- post.excerpt %> <% } else { %> <%- truncate(strip_html(post.content), { length: 150, omission: ' ...' }) %> <% } %> </div> <a class="more" href="<%- url_for(post.path) %>"><%- theme.excerpt_link %></a> </article> <% } %> <% }) %> <% if (page.total > 1){ %> <%- partial('_partial/pager') %> <% } %></main> But according to my test, if this code is added directly, two article lists will appear on the main language homepage; after comparing the two structures, I tried to replace the main part of the original code, but the webpage could not be rendered. Since I currently know nothing about JavaScript, I asked ChatGPT for help and obtained the following code: 123456789101112131415161718192021222324252627282930313233343536373839404142434445464748495051525354555657585960616263<% // Assuming the current language is stored in page.lang or config.languagevar currentLang = page.lang || config.language; // Filter articles that match the current languagevar filteredPosts = page.posts.filter(function(post) { return post.lang === currentLang; // Make sure the article is in the same language as the current one}); %><% filteredPosts.each(function (post) { %> <div class="row mx-auto index-card"> <% var post_url = url_for(post.path), index_img = post.index_img || theme.post.default_index_img %> <% if (index_img) { %> <div class="col-12 col-md-4 m-auto index-img"> <a href="<%= post_url %>" target="<%- theme.index.post_url_target %>"> <img src="<%= url_for(index_img) %>" alt="<%= post.title %>"> </a> </div> <% } %> <article class="col-12 col-md-<%= index_img ? '8' : '12' %> mx-auto index-info"> <h2 class="index-header"> <% if (theme.index.post_sticky && theme.index.post_sticky.enable && post.sticky > 0) { %> <i class="index-pin <%= theme.index.post_sticky && theme.index.post_sticky.icon %>" title="Pin on top"></i> <% } %> <a href="<%= post_url %>" target="<%- theme.index.post_url_target %>"> <%= post.title %> </a> </h2> <% var excerpt = post.description || post.excerpt || (theme.index.auto_excerpt.enable && !post.encrypt && post.content) %> <a class="index-excerpt <%= index_img ? '' : 'index-excerpt__noimg' %>" href="<%= post_url %>" target="<%- theme.index.post_url_target %>"> <div> <%- strip_html(excerpt).substring(0, 200).trim().replace(/\n/g, ' ') %> </div> </a> <div class="index-btm post-metas"> <% if (theme.index.post_meta.date) { %> <div class="post-meta mr-3"> <i class="iconfont icon-date"></i> <time datetime="<%= full_date(post.date, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm') %>" pubdate> <%- date(post.date, config.date_format) %> </time> </div> <% } %> <% if (theme.index.post_meta.category && post.categories.length > 0) { %> <div class="post-meta mr-3 d-flex align-items-center"> <i class="iconfont icon-category"></i> <%- partial('_partials/category-chains', { categories: post.categories, limit: 1 }) %> </div> <% } %> <% if (theme.index.post_meta.tag && post.tags.length > 0) { %> <div class="post-meta"> <i class="iconfont icon-tags"></i> <% post.tags.each(function(tag){ %> <a href="<%= url_for(tag.path) %>">#<%- tag.name %></a> <% }) %> </div> <% } %> </div> </article> </div><% }) %> Simply replace the main part of the original file with the above code to run correctly. ▶“main part” 123456789101112131415161718192021222324252627282930313233343536373839404142434445464748495051525354<% page.posts.each(function (post) { %> <div class="row mx-auto index-card"> <% var post_url = url_for(post.path), index_img = post.index_img || theme.post.default_index_img %> <% if (index_img) { %> <div class="col-12 col-md-4 m-auto index-img"> <a href="<%= post_url %>" target="<%- theme.index.post_url_target %>"> <img src="<%= url_for(index_img) %>" alt="<%= post.title %>"> </a> </div> <% } %> <article class="col-12 col-md-<%= index_img ? '8' : '12' %> mx-auto index-info"> <h2 class="index-header"> <% if (theme.index.post_sticky && theme.index.post_sticky.enable && post.sticky > 0) { %> <i class="index-pin <%= theme.index.post_sticky && theme.index.post_sticky.icon %>" title="Pin on top"></i> <% } %> <a href="<%= post_url %>" target="<%- theme.index.post_url_target %>"> <%= post.title %> </a> </h2> <% var excerpt = post.description || post.excerpt || (theme.index.auto_excerpt.enable && !post.encrypt && post.content) %> <a class="index-excerpt <%= index_img ? '' : 'index-excerpt__noimg' %>" href="<%= post_url %>" target="<%- theme.index.post_url_target %>"> <div> <%- strip_html(excerpt).substring(0, 200).trim().replace(/\n/g, ' ') %> </div> </a> <div class="index-btm post-metas"> <% if (theme.index.post_meta.date) { %> <div class="post-meta mr-3"> <i class="iconfont icon-date"></i> <time datetime="<%= full_date(post.date, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm') %>" pubdate> <%- date(post.date, config.date_format) %> </time> </div> <% } %> <% if (theme.index.post_meta.category && post.categories.length > 0) { %> <div class="post-meta mr-3 d-flex align-items-center"> <i class="iconfont icon-category"></i> <%- partial('_partials/category-chains', { categories: post.categories, limit: 1 }) %> </div> <% } %> <% if (theme.index.post_meta.tag && post.tags.length > 0) { %> <div class="post-meta"> <i class="iconfont icon-tags"></i> <% post.tags.each(function(tag){ %> <a href="<%= url_for(tag.path) %>">#<%- tag.name %></a> <% }) %> </div> <% } %> </div> </article> </div><% }) %> But this solution still has very serious flaws: special pages, such as the about page, cannot be internationalized; in addition, articles in other languages ​​can still be seen on the archive page. Perhaps the hexo-generator-i18n plugin can solve this problem, but I have not yet figured out how it works from the documentation and practice; coupled with concerns about potential problems that may be caused by the modification of the main source code, this solution was quickly abandoned by this site after a short period of use. Written at the endWhen studying the third solution, I could hardly hold back my complaints: Why is the ejs code so dazzling? The internationalization solution of the Hexo framework is still immature, and each theme has its own way, and the Fluid theme unfortunately does not have built-in multilingual content support. I have considered tearing down the entire architecture and starting over, but considering the already huge sunk costs, I chose to stick to it. The current solution is still immature. For example, when switching languages, readers will only be directed to the homepage of the corresponding language, the local maintenance process management lacks systematization, etc. When I come up with a complete solution, I will publish it in this blog. In the process of tossing, I came into contact with a lot of new knowledge, discovered other blogs that insist on updating high-quality content, and found more directions worth exploring (tossing). For example, the previously mentioned automated deployment, access to the Google Translate API, and embedded JavaScript. The new gameplay of the public folder seems to be a very rich mine, but according to the information I have found, this mine does not seem to have attracted people’s attention (after all, why write HTML yourself when you can solve it with a framework). I may publish follow-up research on these topics in the future, hoping to provide some insignificant help to others. This essay was generated with Google Translation. The Simplified Chinese edition prevails if conflicts exist. See the description of the “Languages” section of Fluid official documentation for details: “Setting a different language in the site config will change the text of the theme.” ↩

posts.title: Why I Open the Blog
posts.excerpt: I have always wanted to record my various thoughts and ideas, but for a long time, due to various reasons, I couldn't fulfill this desire. Over time, I realized that many of my earlier reflections and ideas had been buried in the desert of memory, difficult to retrieve. When I finally had a precious moment to open my long-sealed notebook, I found myself lacking in knowledge and creativity. I also noticed how fragmented media had eroded my language skills, leaving me unable to effectively organize my thoughts and complex feelings.
posts.text: Hello World! I have always wanted to record my various thoughts and ideas, but for a long time, due to various reasons, I couldn’t fulfill this desire. Over time, I realized that many of my earlier reflections and ideas had been buried in the desert of memory, difficult to retrieve. When I finally had a precious moment to open my long-sealed notebook, I found myself lacking in knowledge and creativity. I also noticed how fragmented media had eroded my language skills, leaving me unable to effectively organize my thoughts and complex feelings. It feels like the situation has become urgent. I need to continuously produce content—not fragmented, pretentious sentences on various social media platforms, but complete, well-structured articles to document my intellectual journey. That’s why I’ve chosen this “outdated” format for the internet—a blog—to take on this task. Unlike most blogs, this one will not be confined to topics like computer technology, nor will it adhere to any fixed theme. I don’t want the journey it records to be limited to just a few fields. The brilliance of human knowledge across various domains will all be reflected in its milestones. However, because of this, it may lack the rigor I aspire to, and professional readers might even find it simplistic or laughable. Though one person’s knowledge system can hardly cover everything, I sincerely ask my readers to offer valuable guidance, suggestions, and criticisms. On this blog, I will share my knowledge, insights, and thoughts. I hope that through continuous updates, I can push myself to remain on the path of lifelong learning. This is also why the blog is named “Daily Clarity” (每日明). I have some small ambitions as well. Once the blog has taken shape, I might create a community on GitHub to allow everyone to share their views and participate in the enrichment and improvement of this project. If this humble blog can provide even a small amount of help for someone else’s journey, it would mean the world to me. RoadmapFirst, I need to address the technical issues of this blog. I’m currently unfamiliar with the Hexo framework and haven’t decided whether to stick with the current theme. There are also pressing issues like comments and multilingual support that need to be resolved. Next comes updating the blog content. But that’s not a problem—I already know what I want to write for the next post. Finally, once the content becomes substantial, I may create dedicated sections for certain topics to help me stay updated on those areas. I might also write things like “weekly/monthly reports” or “monthly/quarterly/annual specials,” but that’s all for later. This essay was generated with Google Translation. The Simplified Chinese edition prevails if conflicts exist.
